Students who obtain at least 16 ECTS credits in their degree programme per academic year have taken a certain number of exams and are thus considered 'prüfungaktiv’. This is relevant in bachelor’s, diploma and master's programmes. It does not apply to doctoral programmes. Only ECTS credits of courses passed are considered in the calculation. All degree programmes in which students take a certain number of exams are taken into account. - Do all earned ECTS credits count towards examination activity? ECTS credits from recognitions are not taken into account for the calculation. For cooperative degree programmes, only the share of ECTS credits that counts towards examination activity at the University of Vienna is considered. - Why is the number of students who are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ (taking a certain number of exams) important? The more students are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ in all their degree programmes, the more financial resources the University can use for research and teaching. In addition, evidence of having taking a certain number of exams can be a prerequisite for receiving financial aids, such as Austrian family allowance. - What is the difference between the minimum number of credits and the number of students taking a certain number of exams? heading_path: Prüfungsaktiv – examination activity – what does this mean?
retrieved.text: Have you ever come across the phrase ‘prüfungsaktiv’ or ‘number of exams taken’ and wondered what it means? In a nutshell: Several years ago, a new university financing scheme was introduced in Austria. The Republic of Austria provides funding to the public universities and, thus, also funds a large part of your studies. The allocation of financial resources to universities in Austria is based on the number of students being 'prüfungsaktiv' i.e. students taking a certain number of exams at the relevant university. Bachelor’s, master’s and diploma programmes in which a student completes at least 16 ECTS credits or at least 8 SWS within the academic year are considered 'prüfungsaktiv'. In the academic year of 2020/2021, 53,000 students in total took a certain number of exams at the University of Vienna. The target to be achieved is set every three years between the ministry and the university. Every ECTS credit counts: for you personally and for the University of Vienna Taking exams and writing papers is part of studying. They contribute to your personal success and allow you to progress in your studies. If you take a certain number of exams in your degree programme(s), you also contribute to more funding being allocated to the University of Vienna for innovative research and high-quality teaching. Therefore, the University of Vienna strives to ensure that you pass many exams. Your directors of

heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
retrieved.text: At the University of Vienna, an examination is a formal opportunity for students to demonstrate the level of knowledge and skills they have acquired and the achievement of the learning outcomes defined in the curriculum. It is not merely a matter of assessing performance for the award of a grade or academic degree, but also of receiving valuable feedback on learning progress and ensuring fairness and academic integrity. In addition to oral and written examinations, written assignments and presentations also count as examinations. In the current age of AI, the nature of examinations is evolving. The focus is shifting from mere knowledge reproduction to assessing higher levels of competence, such as reflection, transfer and design. Consequently, emphasis is now on both the final outcome and the individual learner's progress and development. Whether and how you may use AI in examinations is determined by the respective examiners. - Where can you find the rules and regulations? The binding provisions specifying which AI tools are permitted for a particular course or examination can be found directly in the course description in u:find. As a general rule, anything not explicitly listed there as permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments,

heading_path: Exemptions for the bachelor's programme in Teacher Education

retrieved.text:
You are exempt from the aptitude test procedure for the bachelor's programme in Teacher Education at the University of Vienna if you have studied or are currently studying one of the following programmes at a university or college of teacher education in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland.

Sie sind vom Eignungsverfahren für das Bachelorstudium Lehramt an der Universität Wien ausgenommen, wenn Sie eines der folgenden Studien an einer Universität oder Pädagogischen Hochschule eines EU/EWR-Landes oder der Schweiz studieren oder abgeschlossen haben.

- Bachelor's programme in Teacher Education for secondary schools (general education)
- Programme in Business Education
- Programme in Religious Education

**Follow these steps:**

1. Create your u:account in [u:space](http://uspace.univie.ac.at/en). Make sure you remember your login data. You will need the u:account during your studies. Log into u:space and complete the registration.
2. Send the following proofs via the [Servicedesk](https://servicedesk.univie.ac.at/plugins/servlet/desk/portal/62/create/935)
   1. all pages of your secondary-school leaving certificate merged into one PDF file
      including translation if the document was not issued in German or English
   2. your current confirmation of studies or your degree certificate
3. State which subjects you want to study in your message. Note: If you want to study the teaching subject in Sports and Physical Education please also send your confirmation of studies to [StudiesServiceCenter Sport Science](#).

- [### What is the VWU?

  The VWU is a preparation programme for university studies. When you apply for a degree programme and we have reviewed your documents, you may be required to take supplementary examinations (e.g. in German, if you do not yet have level C1). You can take these supplementary examinations in the VWU.

  You cannot apply directly for the VWU. Submit your application for admission to a degree programme.

  Info about the VWU](https://vorstudienlehrgang.oead.at/en/vienna)
- [### Important information about the VWU:

  Once you have been admitted to the “University Programme Preparation Programme”, only the supplementary German examination (EPD) of the VWU will be accepted as proof of German language proficiency without exception. In this case, it is not possible to discontinue the VWU and take a German examination at another institution. Failure to comply can have serious consequences for your residence permit.

  Individuals who have discontinued the VWU and have taken another language examination in the meantime can retake the supplementary examination in German.

  Detailed information about this rule](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/admission/german-language-proficiency/detailed-information/)

heading_path: Important note for students in the VWU
retrieved.text:
Once you have been admitted to the University Preparation Programme of the Vienna Universities as a non-degree student, only the supplementary examination in German (Ergänzungsprüfung Deutsch, EPD)  is accepted as a proof of German language proficiency! It is not possible to leave the preparation programme and take a German language exam elsewhere. Disregard may lead to severe consequences such as the loss of your residence permit.

Persons who have quit the VWU and have passed a different German language exam in the meanwhile can still take the supplementary examination in German (EPD).

[Detailed information on the regulation](/en/admission/german-language-proficiency/detailed-information/)

While you are admitted to the non-degree programme "attending the VWU" ("Besuch des Vorstudienlehrgangs"), you are only allowed to attend courses in the VWU and complete the prescribed supplementary examinations there. Taking exams from the degree programme is not allowed.

heading_path: Prüfungsaktiv – examination activity – what does this mean?
reranked.text: Have you ever come across the phrase ‘prüfungsaktiv’ or ‘number of exams taken’ and wondered what it means? In a nutshell: Several years ago, a new university financing scheme was introduced in Austria. The Republic of Austria provides funding to the public universities and, thus, also funds a large part of your studies. The allocation of financial resources to universities in Austria is based on the number of students being 'prüfungsaktiv' i.e. students taking a certain number of exams at the relevant university. Bachelor’s, master’s and diploma programmes in which a student completes at least 16 ECTS credits or at least 8 SWS within the academic year are considered 'prüfungsaktiv'. In the academic year of 2020/2021, 53,000 students in total took a certain number of exams at the University of Vienna. The target to be achieved is set every three years between the ministry and the university. Every ECTS credit counts: for you personally and for the University of Vienna Taking exams and writing papers is part of studying. They contribute to your personal success and allow you to progress in your studies. If you take a certain number of exams in your degree programme(s), you also contribute to more funding being allocated to the University of Vienna for innovative research and high-quality teaching. heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
reranked.text: At the University of Vienna, an examination is a formal opportunity for students to demonstrate the level of knowledge and skills they have acquired and the achievement of the learning outcomes defined in the curriculum. It is not merely a matter of assessing performance for the award of a grade or academic degree, but also of receiving valuable feedback on learning progress and ensuring fairness and academic integrity. In addition to oral and written examinations, written assignments and presentations also count as examinations. In the current age of AI, the nature of examinations is evolving. The focus is shifting from mere knowledge reproduction to assessing higher levels of competence, such as reflection, transfer and design. Consequently, emphasis is now on both the final outcome and the individual learner's progress and development. Whether and how you may use AI in examinations is determined by the respective examiners. - Where can you find the rules and regulations? Studying in the University Preparation Programme of the Vienna Universities (VWU)
heading_path: Important note for students in the VWU
reranked.text:
Once you have been admitted to the University Preparation Programme of the Vienna Universities as a non-degree student, only the supplementary examination in German (Ergänzungsprüfung Deutsch, EPD)  is accepted as a proof of German language proficiency! It is not possible to leave the preparation programme and take a German language exam elsewhere. Disregard may lead to severe consequences such as the loss of your residence permit.

Persons who have quit the VWU and have passed a different German language exam in the meanwhile can still take the supplementary examination in German (EPD).

[Detailed information on the regulation](/en/admission/german-language-proficiency/detailed-information/)

While you are admitted to the non-degree programme "attending the VWU" ("Besuch des Vorstudienlehrgangs"), you are only allowed to attend courses in the VWU and complete the prescribed supplementary examinations there. Taking exams from the degree programme is not allowed.

selected.text: exams. # FAQs about being 'prüfungsaktiv' (examination activity) / - When are students ‘prüfungsaktiv’? Students who obtain at least 16 ECTS credits in their degree programme per academic year have taken a certain number of exams and are thus considered 'prüfungaktiv’. This is relevant in bachelor’s, diploma and master's programmes. It does not apply to doctoral programmes. Only ECTS credits of courses passed are considered in the calculation. All degree programmes in which students take a certain number of exams are taken into account. - Do all earned ECTS credits count towards examination activity? ECTS credits from recognitions are not taken into account for the calculation. For cooperative degree programmes, only the share of ECTS credits that counts towards examination activity at the University of Vienna is considered. - Why is the number of students who are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ (taking a certain number of exams) important? The more students are ‘prüfungsaktiv’ in all their degree programmes, the more financial resources the University can use for research and teaching. In addition, evidence of having taking a certain number of exams can be a prerequisite for receiving financial aids, such as Austrian family allowance. - What is the difference between the minimum number of credits and the number of students taking a certain number of exams? selected.title: FAQs about being 'prüfungsaktiv' (examination activity)
heading_path: Prüfungsaktiv – examination activity – what does this mean?
selected.text: Have you ever come across the phrase ‘prüfungsaktiv’ or ‘number of exams taken’ and wondered what it means? In a nutshell: Several years ago, a new university financing scheme was introduced in Austria. The Republic of Austria provides funding to the public universities and, thus, also funds a large part of your studies. The allocation of financial resources to universities in Austria is based on the number of students being 'prüfungsaktiv' i.e. students taking a certain number of exams at the relevant university. Bachelor’s, master’s and diploma programmes in which a student completes at least 16 ECTS credits or at least 8 SWS within the academic year are considered 'prüfungsaktiv'. In the academic year of 2020/2021, 53,000 students in total took a certain number of exams at the University of Vienna. The target to be achieved is set every three years between the ministry and the university. Every ECTS credit counts: for you personally and for the University of Vienna Taking exams and writing papers is part of studying. They contribute to your personal success and allow you to progress in your studies. If you take a certain number of exams in your degree programme(s), you also contribute to more funding being allocated to the University of Vienna for innovative research and high-quality teaching. Therefore, the University of Vienna strives to ensure that you pass many exams. Your directors of

If you do not take a certain number of exams, it does not have any consequences for you personally in terms of study law. Students can have various reasons why they temporarily take fewer exams: gainful employment, family duties and care obligations. - If you are not able to take exams for a certain time due to personal reasons, you can apply for a [leave of absence](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/admission/leave-of-absence/). - Also students enrolled in more than one degree programme often take fewer exams. If you no longer actively study one or several of your degree programmes, you can [close the relevant degree programme](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/en/admission/closing-a-degree-programmes/). - Please also notify the University if you [discontinue exams or courses for an important reason](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/abbruch-von-pruefungen-oder-lehrveranstaltungen-aus-wichtigem-grund/).
